intro:
Regeneration (Greek palingenesia, “new birth” or “rebirth”; described also as being “born again,” “born from above,” or “born of the Spirit”) is the act of God by which he imparts new spiritual life to those who are by nature spiritually dead. It is not reformation of the old self but the gift of a new one — a sovereign, instantaneous, life-giving work of the Holy Spirit at the root of conversion. Jesus presses its necessity on Nicodemus: “unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God … unless one is born of water and the Spirit, he cannot enter the kingdom of God” (John 3:3-5). Like the wind, the Spirit “blows where it wishes,” and so does this birth — perceptible in its effects, hidden in its origin (John 3:8).

The new birth answers a need diagnosed from the beginning. After the Fall the human heart is set on evil (Genesis 6:5), and the law could expose sin but not create the obedience it demanded. So the prophets promised an inward work of God himself: a circumcised heart that loves the LORD (Deuteronomy 30:6), a new heart and a new spirit in place of the heart of stone (Ezekiel 36:26-27), the law written within (Jeremiah 31:33). David’s prayer, “Create in me a clean heart, O God” (Psalm 51:10), reaches for the very renewal the gospel supplies.

The New Testament shows that renewal accomplished. Those born again are “born, not of blood nor of the will of the flesh nor of the will of man, but of God” (John 1:13) — regeneration is monergistic, God’s initiative, not man’s achievement. The Spirit works through the living word of the gospel, the “imperishable seed” by which we are born again (1 Peter 1:23; James 1:18). Paul names it directly: God “saved us … by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit” (Titus 3:5), making those “dead in … trespasses” alive together with Christ (Ephesians 2:4-5). The regenerate person is a “new creation; the old has passed away … the new has come” (2 Corinthians 5:17), having passed “out of death into life” (1 John 3:14).

Regeneration is thus the hidden source of all that follows in salvation: it gives rise to saving faith and repentance, and bears fruit in love, obedience, and victory over sin — “everyone who has been born of God” does not make a practice of sinning and overcomes the world (1 John 5:4; 1 John 5:18). Reformed theology has generally held that regeneration logically precedes faith (the dead must be made alive before they can believe), while Arminian theology more often treats faith as its condition; both confess that the new birth is finally God’s gracious work. The same word palingenesia also names the cosmic renewal at the end — “the new world” when the Son of Man sits on his glorious throne (Matthew 19:28) — so that the rebirth of the believer is the firstfruits of the rebirth of all things.
